content.problemStatement:
Chef Ada has a matrix with NNN rows (numbered 111 through NNN from top to bottom) and NNN columns (numbered 111 through NNN from left to right) containing all integers between 111 and N2N^2N2 inclusive. For each valid iii and jjj, let's denote the cell in the iii-th row and jjj-th column by (i,j)(i,j)(i,j).

Ada wants to sort the matrix in row-major order ― for each valid iii and jjj, she wants the cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) to contain the value (i−1)⋅N+j(i-1) \cdot N + j(i−1)⋅N+j.

In one operation, Ada should choose an integer LLL and transpose the submatrix between rows 111 and LLL and columns 111 and LLL (inclusive). Formally, for each iii and jjj (1≤i,j≤L1 \le i, j \le L1≤i,j≤L), the value in the cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) after this operation is equal to the value in (j,i)(j, i)(j,i) before it.

The initial state of the matrix is chosen in such a way that it is possible to sort it using a finite number of operations (possibly zero). Find the smallest number of operations required to sort the matrix.
constraints: The matrix contains each integer from 1 to N² exactly once.
The sum of N² over all test files does not exceed 3·10⁵.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N (4 ≤ N ≤ 64). Then N lines follow, each containing N space‑separated integers – the initial matrix.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum number of prefix‑transpose operations required to transform the matrix into row‑major order.

sampleTestCases.explanation: All diagonal elements already contain their target values, so no operation is needed.
Only the element at (4,4) is wrong (15 instead of 16). One transpose with L=4 fixes it.
Diagonal mismatches: (3,3)=14 (should be 13) and (5,5)=24 (should be 25). Operations with L=5 and then L=3 resolve both.

solution.approach: The only operation allowed is transposing the top‑left L×L sub‑matrix. After such a transpose, the element that should finally be at position (i,i) (value (i‑1)·N + i) will be correct if and only if it was already correct before the operation. Therefore we can process the matrix from the largest index downwards: for i = N … 1, if the current element at (i,i) is not the target value, we must perform an operation with L = i, which fixes the i‑th row and column simultaneously. Each such mismatch requires exactly one operation and fixing a larger L never harms already fixed smaller diagonals. Hence the answer equals the number of indices i for which a[i][i] ≠ (i‑1)·N + i when scanning from N down to 1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N²) per test case – reading the matrix dominates; the diagonal check is O(N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N²) to store the matrix (can be reduced to O(N) by reading row by row, but O(N²) fits the limits).
